scope.b1: Scope answers the question "what will the project do (and NOT do)?" Poor scope control is the #1 cause of project failure.
scope.b2: ENProject Scope — the work performed to deliver a product, service, or result with the specified features and functions.
scope.b3: ENProduct Scope — the features and functions that characterize a product, service, or result.
scope.b4:
Product ScopeProject Scope
What it isThe features & functions of the productThe work required to create that product
Completion measured byProduct requirementsThe Project Management Plan
scope.b6: ENScope Creep — the uncontrolled expansion to product or project scope without adjustments to time, cost, and resources.
scope.b7: ENGold Plating — adding extra features or work not requested by the customer, beyond the agreed requirements.
scope.b8:
Scope CreepUncontrolled growth — the customer/team adds work without going through change control. "Creep" = it slowly spreads.
Gold PlatingThe team adds features the customer did not ask for ("gold plating"). Wasteful, adds no value.
scope.b9: Memory tip · Creep vs Gold PlatingScope Creep = pushed in by others (sneaky, uncontrolled change). Gold Plating = the TEAM adds it (well-meant but unnecessary). Both are bad — always stick to change control.
scope.b10:
WBS — work decomposition tree (3 levels)
Level 1 · Project — "Sales app"
Level 2 · Deliverable — Module A (Cart)
Level 3 · WP A1 · UI designLevel 3 · WP A2 · Cart API
Level 2 · Deliverable — Module B (Payment)
Level 3 · WP B1 · Payment gatewayLevel 3 · WP B2 · Invoicing
scope.b11:
Work Package → Activities → Milestone
WBS · ScopeWP A2 · Cart API
›
ActivityDesign API
ActivityBuild API
ActivityTest API
›
🏁 MilestoneModule A done · Dur = 0
A Work Package is broken into Activities when scheduling (Define Activities); a Milestone is a marker with zero duration
scope.b12: WBS vs Activity vs MilestoneWBS (part of Scope) decomposes down to the Work Package — answers "WHAT". An Activity (part of Schedule, created in Define Activities) is a smaller task to execute a work package — answers "HOW / WHEN". A Milestone = a key marker with duration = 0 (e.g. "Module A done", "contract signed").
scope.b13: ENWork Breakdown Structure (WBS) — a hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team to accomplish the project objectives and create the required deliverables.
scope.b14: ENWork Package — the work defined at the lowest level of the WBS for which cost and duration can be estimated and managed.
scope.b15: ENScope Baseline — the approved version of the scope statement, WBS, and its associated WBS dictionary, changed only through formal change control.
scope.b16: WBS = the "work decomposition tree": break the whole scope into manageable pieces. The WBS says WHAT (not how/when). The lowest element of the WBS is called a Work Package.
scope.b17:
• Scope Baseline = 3 components: Scope Statement + WBS + WBS Dictionary (which describes each element in detail).
  • 100% Rule: the sum of the "children" = 100% of the "parent"; nothing extra, nothing missing.
  • 8/80 Rule: a work package should take 8 to 80 hours (not too small, not too large).
  • RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix): links each requirement to its objective & deliverable, tracked across the whole life cycle.
scope.b18: Validate Scope ≠ Control QualityControl Quality → produces Verified deliverables (QC checks correct/incorrect — internal). Validate Scope → produces Accepted deliverables (customer/sponsor sign off — formal). Order: check quality first, customer accepts after.
scope.b19: Memory tip · Verified vs AcceptedVerified = "is it correct?" (internal QC). Accepted = "did the customer sign off?" Also MoSCoW to prioritise requirements: Must · Should · Could · Won't-this-time.
scope.b20:
Sample · Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)
IDRequirementBusiness goalDeliverable / WBSTest caseStatus
R-01Add product to cartIncrease conversionWP A2 · Cart APITC-11Verified
R-02Domestic payment (VNPay)Online revenueModule B · PaymentTC-20In progress
R-03Issue e-invoiceTax complianceWP B2 · InvoicingTC-25New
scope.b22: ENRequirement — a condition or capability needed by a stakeholder to solve a problem or achieve an objective, or that must be met by a solution to satisfy a contract, standard, or specification.
scope.b23:
Requirement typeDescription
Business requirementsHigh-level needs of the organization: why the project exists, business goals.
Stakeholder requirementsNeeds of a specific stakeholder or group.
Solution requirementsWhat the product needs; includes Functional (what it does) & Non-functional (performance, security, reliability…).
Transition requirementsTemporary capabilities to move from the old to the new state (training, data migration).
Project requirementsHow the project should run: time, cost, process.
Quality requirementsConditions to confirm a deliverable meets the standard.
scope.b25:
InterviewsOne-on-one interviews (formal/informal) to draw information from stakeholders.
BrainstormingGather many ideas, no criticism, welcome "wild" ideas, then combine & improve.
Nominal Group TechniqueBrainstorm, then vote to rank ideas → prioritise (an upgrade of brainstorming).
Delphi TechniqueAnonymously poll a panel of experts over several rounds until consensus — avoids bias & peer pressure.
Prototype / WireframeA working model / interface sketch so the customer can "see it early" & give feedback.
Observation (Job Shadowing)Watch users at work to uncover hidden requirements they don't state.
Questionnaires & SurveysQuestionnaires to quickly gather from a large audience.
BenchmarkingCompare with other organizations/projects to find best practices & improvement ideas.
Document AnalysisAnalyse existing documents: contracts, current processes, RFPs, business rules…
scope.b26: Memory tip · DelphiDelphi = anonymous + multiple rounds + experts. The key is anonymity so no one is pressured by the boss/crowd → less bias.
scope.b28:
MoSCoWMust have · Should have · Could have · Won't have this time — priority tiers for requirements.
Kano Model3 groups: Basic (missing = dissatisfied) · Performance (more = happier) · Delighter/Exciter (pleasant surprise). Don't spend on delighters before basics are met.
100-Point / Multi-criteriaDistribute 100 points or score on multiple criteria to rank requirements.
Context DiagramThe system in the middle, external actors/systems & data flows — sets the scope boundary.
scope.b29: Don't confuse · Configuration vs Change ManagementConfiguration Management = managing the product/deliverable specification (versions, technical baseline — "what the product looks like & contains"). Change Management = the process for handling change requests (propose → assess impact → approve via CCB). The PMP exam loves to swap these two.
scope.b30: ENProduct Analysis — a technique for defining scope that translates high-level product descriptions into tangible deliverables by asking questions about the product's use, characteristics, and other relevant aspects.
scope.b31: Used in Define Scope to turn a high-level product description into concrete deliverables. Methods: Product Breakdown, Systems analysis, Requirements analysis, Systems engineering, Value engineering.
scope.b32: What the WBS is & is NOTThe WBS is NOT a full task list, not a schedule, not an org chart. The WBS is a complete classification of the scope: it says WHAT will be done (not how/when). Plan by deliverable (result), not by action.
